This puts other staff
and other residents under extreme pressure and real risk.
We need Health New Zealand to plan for capacity,
not just nationally but regionally
and to work with providers to make sure
that beds are available when and where people need them.
This could be achieved by strengthening clauses 13 and 14.
We support stronger planning and monitoring in this bill,
but it needs teeth.
We'd like to see Health New Zealand required under clause 23
to publish regional arc capacity plans every year,
report on median wait times
and show the number of hospital nights saved
through arc placements.
That transparency will let Parliament
and the public see whether we are moving
in the right direction.
And yet we know there's one thing
that's really missing here, Mr.
Chair, inside this bill, it's completely absent.
So we know that a 100% of our citizens
and residents in this country will die.
Yet this bill completely misses the opportunity to ensure
that palliative care is explicitly recognized as a core part
of the health system that Health New Zealand must
plan for and deliver.
Palliative care is essential, not just at the end of life,
but across the continuum of care for people with complex
and life limiting conditions.
It relieves pressures on hospitals, supports families
and whanau, and allows people to die with dignity,
often closer to home.
We encourage the committee
to consider inserting a specific reference
to palliative care within the bill's objectives
and planning requirements so that access, capacity
and service quality are measured and improved over time.
So in summary, in our context, this bill is a good step,
but we suggest you be bolder.
Older New Zealanders must be explicitly recognized.
Infrastructure investment must include ARC
as critical health infrastructure and sys.
And the system must be held accountable for ensuring
that older people can access care close to home, connected
to whanau and when they need it.
That's how we'll free up hospital capacity support seven day
discharge and deliver on the promise
of Piora healthy futures for everyone.
Thank you for the opportunity to speak to you today.
I am happy to take any questions.
Thank you Chay, we've got question from Johana.
Uh, thank you Auntie Tracy. I'm Mahi.
Um, I'd like to recognize your work and advocacy
and being our for the inquiry into aged care facilities
and cognitive disorders, um,
but also your targeted recommendations that you've provided
because it sets out, you know, some tangible things
that we can consider in terms of our recommendations.
Um, the issue around equity, timely
and quality health service.
You've kind of introduced some of those issues in terms
of some rural populations.
Do you think that there are urban spaces too that we have,
um, gaps that you are seeing in terms of the provision
of these ARC services, both rural as well as urban?
Absolutely. So that they're both equity issues,
but they're both, they both manifest in different ways.
So in our urban areas, we just don't have the capacity.
I was in Auckland last week speaking to, um, a lot of, uh,
providers up there in Auckland
and um, if you can't afford a premium
or an occupational rights um, agreement,
then you have got a really hard job finding somewhere
that when you've been assessed as needing care,
because let's be, let's be honest, nobody chooses
to go into residential care.
They need to go into residential care
because they require the level of support.
So in an urban area, there's just lack
of capacity in a rural area.
I think possibly it's even more cruel, which is
that we are separating families and loved ones.
I'll give you an example of a gentleman on the west coast
whose wife, they've been married something like
60, 65 years.
His wife suffers from dementia
and requires a secure care facility.
The closest secure care facility is four hours
away every morning.
This gentleman who they're both in their eighties,
this gentleman gets up and drives to see the woman he loves.
He will not stop doing that,
but he is driven off the road three times
already during this trip.
So we are doing that to families, we are doing that
to couples and we can do better.
Sure. Thank you Tracy for your submission
and thank you for your continued advocacy
to our senior visit resident as well.
Um, your hard work is, is uh, uh, we,
we admire your hard work.
Um, so I think this is for now, I think we got a break, uh,
lunch break for now and we will see, uh, if one back to the,
uh, to the public meeting at one o'clock.
